danger_signals.meaning: Collision hazard that significantly slows player progress
Running out of fuel causes race termination; resource scarcity as threat
Reduced visibility and increased difficulty; environmental challenge state

safety_signals.meaning: Safe zone to refuel and manage resources, though entry costs time
Clear visibility and baseline difficulty; safer racing environment

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Four distinct car models (Cannibal, Sidewinder, and two others) with different visual appearances, handling characteristics, and performance profiles
Four-course regional circuits across multiple countries; distinct track environments serve as visual faction/location identity
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Regional track design and landscape differentiation (Vegas track noted as distinct musical/visual environment)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Four-course regional circuits with cumulative ranking system displayed
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must finish within top five positions to advance; competitive intensity increases per region

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Bright, clear track visibility with standard environmental rendering
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Baseline difficulty; optimal racing conditions for player navigation and obstacle avoidance

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Darkened track environment with reduced lighting
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Increased hazard difficulty; player must navigate with compromised visibility as world-state indicator
Time pressure increases; pit stop becomes mandatory refuge; resource scarcity manifests as environmental threat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Transition to new regional circuits and track environments
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Competitive field and track hazards intensify; world state shifts from qualification to championship pursuit

environmental_storytelling.example: Vegas track and other named regions communicate international racing circuit progression; each region's visual design conveys world geography and racing culture
Day/night race cycles communicate temporal progression and environmental challenge scaling without narrative exposition

Car selection screen showing Cannibal (high-speed, low-grip) vs Sidewinder (superior cornering, lower fuel consumption) communicates distinct driving philosophies and playstyle implications through mechanical/visual presentation
Pit stop mechanic and fuel gauge communicate resource scarcity and strategic decision-making as core world-state element

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Prioritizes velocity over stability; requires skilled fuel management; high consumption rate manifests in aggressive driving pressure
Prioritizes cornering precision and efficiency; lower fuel consumption allows extended race segments without pit stops; manifests as strategic advantage in technical courses
Compete for top-five positions; positioning on track communicates relative threat level and world-state competitiveness
Temporary speed burst mechanic signals risk/reward decision point; limited availability communicates resource scarcity and strategic timing requirement

notes: Top Gear is a pure racing simulation with minimal narrative or faction complexity. World-state communication relies entirely on visual/mechanical systems: environmental conditions (day/night), resource management (fuel), car selection properties, and competitive ranking. The game manifests world-state through track design variety (regional differentiation), hazard visualization (obstacles, darkness), and resource depletion mechanics. No explicit faction systems, political situations, or danger/safety signaling beyond environmental hazards. The paradigm is mechanically-driven rather than narratively-driven; visual communication serves gameplay clarity rather than worldbuilding.
